subject: College Medicine
question: Which of the following is a feature of X-linked dominant inheritance?

options.D: Transmitted by males only to females

analysis: Step 1: Evaluate Choice A (Parental consanguinity): Consanguinity increases the probability of homozygosity for rare alleles and is a classic feature of autosomal recessive inheritance, not X-linked dominant inheritance, so this choice is incorrect.

Step 2: Evaluate Choice B (Male to male transmission): In X-linked inheritance, fathers transmit their Y chromosome to sons and their X chromosome to daughters; therefore, an affected father cannot pass an X-linked trait to his son, ruling out male-to-male transmission as a feature of X-linked dominant inheritance.

Step 3: Evaluate Choice C (Transmission only by females): This statement is false because both affected males and affected females can transmit X-linked dominant traits; affected heterozygous females transmit the mutation to approximately 50% of their sons and daughters, meaning transmission is not exclusive to females.

Step 4: Evaluate Choice D (Transmitted by males only to females): An affected male possesses one X chromosome carrying the dominant mutation and one Y chromosome; he will inevitably pass his mutated X chromosome to all of his daughters (making them affected) and his normal Y chromosome to all of his sons (leaving them unaffected), making this a definitive and diagnostic feature of X-linked dominant inheritance.

Step 5: Based on the sequential evaluation, Choices A, B, and C describe patterns inconsistent with X-linked dominant genetics, while Choice D accurately captures the obligate transmission rule where affected fathers pass the trait exclusively to their daughters, confirming it as the correct answer.
